screens.body: Case data: four paired readings from your two sensors give a sample covariance matrix $\Sigma = \begin{pmatrix}4 & 2\\2 & 1\end{pmatrix}$. **Step 1 — eigenvalues**: trace $=4+1=5$, determinant $=4\times1-2\times2=0$, so $\lambda^2-5\lambda+0=0$, giving $\lambda(\lambda-5)=0$: eigenvalues $\lambda_1=5$ and $\lambda_2=0$. **Step 2 — principal eigenvector angle**: $\theta=\tfrac12\arctan\!\big(\tfrac{2b}{a-c}\big)=\tfrac12\arctan\!\big(\tfrac{4}{3}\big)\approx\tfrac12(53.1°)\approx26.6°$. **Step 3 — interpretation**: one eigenvalue is exactly zero, meaning *all* the variance lives along the single direction at $26.6°$ — the two sensors' readings are perfectly linearly related (this is a degenerate, perfectly-correlated case, deliberately chosen to make the arithmetic land cleanly). In a real, noisy case this second eigenvalue would be small but not exactly zero, and the ratio between the two eigenvalues would tell you how close to that perfectly-correlated situation your actual data sits.
screens.selfExplainPrompt: In your own words: what does it mean, physically, for one eigenvalue of a covariance matrix to come out exactly zero?
screens.selfExplainAnswer: A strong answer says a zero eigenvalue means there is no variance at all in the direction of the corresponding eigenvector — every data point, no matter which one you pick, lies exactly on the line defined by the other (nonzero-eigenvalue) eigenvector. In this sensor-pair case, that means the two sensors' readings are perfectly, deterministically related to each other with no independent variation of their own — the two-dimensional data cloud has collapsed onto a single line.

screens.worked: Reminder of the method: for $\Sigma = \begin{pmatrix}a & b\\b & c\end{pmatrix}$, find eigenvalues from $\lambda^2-(a+c)\lambda+(ac-b^2)=0$, and the principal eigenvector's angle from $\theta=\tfrac12\arctan\!\big(\tfrac{2b}{a-c}\big)$.
screens.prompt: A new sensor pair gives $\Sigma = \begin{pmatrix}3 & 0\\0 & 3\end{pmatrix}$ (equal variances, zero covariance — completely uncorrelated, equal-strength sensors). What is the larger eigenvalue?
screens.successFeedback: Right — trace = 6, determinant = 9, so λ² − 6λ + 9 = 0 factors as (λ−3)² = 0: both eigenvalues equal 3. Every direction has the same variance — a perfect circle of a data cloud, with no preferred direction at all, exactly what zero covariance and equal variances predicts.
hints: λ² − 6λ + 9 = 0 factors as a perfect square: (λ − 3)² = 0.
